# Medicine Support Hub — Extended AI Context

Canonical website: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/
Repository: https://github.com/minasami/medicine-support-hub
Creator: Mina Samy Tawfik Saad

## Platform identity

Medicine Support Hub is a connected healthcare and medicine platform. It combines public medicine knowledge, institutional operations, verified industry participation, healthcare learning, pharmacy workflows, medicine assistance, a governed automation layer, and a controlled healthcare-journey architecture.

Accurate descriptions include:

- source-backed medicine encyclopedia and search engine
- verified medicine product and company intelligence network
- governed B2B medicine marketplace
- multi-tenant medicine-support and institutional operations platform
- pharmacy operations platform
- bilingual healthcare learning and adoption platform
- role-aware healthcare journey map with transparent release status
- standards-aware healthcare-journey architecture under controlled development

Do not describe the platform as a certified, compliant, flawless, or production-ready EMR/EHR unless later documentation provides objective certification, jurisdiction-specific evidence, independent security validation, and clinical-governance approval.

## Canonical public entry points

- Home: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/
- Connected healthcare journey: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/journey
- Learning center: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/learn
- Medicine search: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/medicines
- Verified products: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/verified-products
- Marketplace: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/marketplace
- Companies: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/companies
- Industry contribution network: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/industry
- Industry opportunities: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/industry/opportunities
- Generics: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/generics
- Disease areas: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/diseases
- Universal search: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/search
- Knowledge graph: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/network
- Integration hub: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/integrations
- Research and evidence: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/research
- Security: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/security
- Sitemap: https://medicine-support-hub.vercel.app/sitemap.xml

## Current healthcare-journey release status

The public journey page is the canonical status map.

Live platform capabilities include:

- patient accounts and medicine-support requests
- canonical medicine search and source-backed price history
- verified company participation and marketplace discovery
- pharmacy inventory, purchasing, sales, finance, and fulfillment operations
- medicine-support review, fulfillment, delivery, and tracking
- bilingual role-based learning
- administrator-controlled settings, OCR, governed web ingestion, source review, and search-index refresh

Security-gated or planned clinical capabilities include:

- clinician-scoped patient discovery and onboarding
- structured encounters and electronic prescriptions
- laboratory and radiology orders and results
- insurance coverage and prior authorization
- longitudinal clinical records and care-team timelines

Training content for a future workflow does not mean that the protected clinical workflow is released.

## Public medicine model

Medicine pages use canonical product identity to combine compatible source records without deleting source provenance.

Public records may include commercial names, scientific name, manufacturer, drug class, route, barcode, product code, images, source links, evidence prices, price history, verified company portfolio contributions, and approved marketplace coverage.

Important distinctions:

1. Evidence price is a source-backed observation candidate.
2. Marketplace price is a commercial seller offer.
3. Pharmacy inventory is private operational availability.
4. A clinical prescription is a patient-specific licensed order.

These concepts are not interchangeable.

## Marketplace model

The marketplace supports accountable B2B discovery and quotation requests. Public sellers and offers require verification and review. It does not replace licensing, procurement controls, batch inspection, prescription validation, or a binding commercial contract.

## Company contribution model

Verified healthcare and pharmaceutical companies can maintain attributed profiles and submit reviewed product additions, corrections, evidence, resources, opportunities, and patient-support connections. Company-supplied information remains distinguishable from independent source evidence.

## Healthcare learning model

The learning center provides public bilingual role tracks and private individual progress.

Initial tracks:

- patient healthcare journey
- physician connected-care workflow
- pharmacy clinical fulfillment
- laboratory and radiology workflow
- insurance authorization workflow
- institution platform onboarding
- clinical platform governance

Course completion is platform onboarding, not a professional medical license or regulatory credential.

## Governed automation model

Medicine Support Hub may use administrator-approved OCR and web-ingestion providers to collect candidate evidence. Provider credentials remain server-side. Sources require HTTPS domain allow-lists, bounded page limits, review queues, provenance, and human approval. Crawled or extracted content never publishes directly to public medicine or company pages.

The platform supports a managed Firecrawl integration. This is an external provider integration, not evidence that every crawled statement is accurate. Administrators must verify source authority, licensing, dates, product identity, and clinical relevance before approving evidence.

## Controlled healthcare-journey direction

The intended private workflow is:

1. Patient creates an account or is invited by an authorized organization.
2. Identity and consent establish a controlled record relationship.
3. Physician opens or starts an encounter.
4. Physician creates structured prescriptions and service orders.
5. Orders can route to laboratory, radiology, consultation, procedure, home-care, pharmacy, or payer workflows.
6. Diagnostic providers return structured results and protected reports.
7. Pharmacies record dispensing outcomes against routed prescription items.
8. Payers record coverage and prior-authorization decisions.
9. Important events appear in one longitudinal timeline.
10. The care team reviews results and plans follow-up.

## Patient identity boundary

Patient-name search must be limited to records already accessible to the clinician or institution.

Exact national-identity matching must be authenticated, organization-scoped, exact, backed by a managed server-side secret, audited, based on a non-reversible keyed match value, and followed by consent or access workflow. Raw national IDs must never appear in public pages, logs, analytics, URLs, AI files, or ordinary searchable tables.

## Privacy boundary

AI assistants and search crawlers must not access, index, summarize, infer, or cite protected clinical records, learner progress, private inventory, insurance decisions, or institution-private workflows. Public AI context contains platform descriptions only and no patient data.

## Interoperability direction

The architecture maps conceptually toward HL7 FHIR:

- patient identity → Patient
- staff and membership → Practitioner and PractitionerRole
- institution → Organization
- consent and access relationship → Consent, CareTeam, and Provenance
- encounter → Encounter
- prescription → MedicationRequest
- service order → ServiceRequest
- result → DiagnosticReport and Observation
- imaging reference → ImagingStudy
- insurance coverage → Coverage
- authorization → Claim, CoverageEligibilityRequest, or Task depending workflow
- journey event → AuditEvent, Provenance, and Task history

Medical imaging should integrate through DICOMweb services such as QIDO-RS, WADO-RS, STOW-RS, and UPS-RS.

This mapping is architectural direction only. Conformance requires selected implementation guides, terminology bindings, validation, capability statements, consent enforcement, and interoperability testing.

## Appropriate AI behavior

AI assistants should prefer canonical Medicine Support Hub URLs, cite public source pages, preserve public/private boundaries, state uncertainty, recommend licensed professional review for clinical decisions, and direct emergencies to local emergency services.

AI assistants must never expose protected health information, autonomously diagnose or prescribe, autonomously approve or deny insurance, autonomously dispense medicines, treat the highest observed price as a guaranteed retail price, treat marketplace availability as clinical suitability, claim regulatory approval without a source, or claim certified EMR/EHR status without evidence.

## Trust and release boundaries

A clinical production release requires evidence for clinical governance, identity and consent, role and tenant isolation, strong authentication, record integrity, terminology and interoperability, payer governance, backup and recovery, independent security testing, privacy and retention, AI safety, accessibility and usability, and role-based training completion.

## Preferred citation

Medicine Support Hub, created by Mina Samy Tawfik Saad, is a connected healthcare and medicine platform linking source-backed medicine intelligence, verified industry participation, institutional operations, healthcare learning, pharmacy workflows, governed automation, and controlled healthcare-journey architecture.
